topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a small, insistent vibration against the smooth glass of my phone screen. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched, a shallow catch in my chest. I stared at the screen, the familiar contours of his profile picture mocking me with their normalcy. He had tagged me in a recent departmental post, a photo of a lecture hall, and now, a private message. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, the keys feeling heavy and unresponsive. A knot tightened low in my stomach, a familiar, suffocating pressure. I wanted to delete the app, to erase the digital footprint that felt suddenly too large, too exposed. I typed a single, hesitant question, the words tasting like ash before I hit send, a silent plea for the connection to simply cease.

[story 2]

I spent the entire afternoon tracing the trajectory of the message, the cursor blinking mockingly on the screen. Every time I looked up, my gaze snagged on the windowpane, watching the relentless drizzle smear the city lights into streaks of blurred color. My shoulders slumped, a slow, deliberate sinking into the worn armchair. It felt like the world had narrowed down to this small, illuminated rectangle, a trap I hadn't anticipated. When Mr. Harrison’s reply finally appeared, it was brief, professionally cordial, and utterly devoid of the warmth I desperately craved. I reread it three times, my jaw clenching until my molars ached. I pushed the phone face-down onto the desk, the sudden silence amplifying the frantic drumming inside my own ears.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive monotony that mirrored the state of my own existence. I kept glancing at my phone, the screen a beacon of unwelcome attention. It wasn't just the likes or the comments; it was the direct, unsolicited outreach from the professor who held the keys to my academic future. A wave of profound inertia washed over me, making my limbs feel strangely weighted, as if moving required an impossible expenditure of energy. I picked up my pen, but the tip dragged across the paper uselessly. I just sat there, my eyes fixed on the screen, waiting for a signal that never came, feeling utterly paralyzed by the sudden, unwelcome visibility of my private life.
